coredata.dc:title: Milk-derived multi-fluorescent graphene quantum dot-based cancer theranostic system
coredata.dc:creator: Thakur, Mukeshchand
Mewada, Ashmi
Pandey, Sunil
Bhori, Mustansir
Singh, Kanchanlata
Sharon, Maheshwar
Sharon, Madhuri
coredata.dc:description: An economical green-chemistry approach was used for the synthesis of aqueous soluble graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from cow milk for simultaneous imaging and drug delivery in cancer. The GQDs synthesized using one-pot microwave-assisted heating were multi-fluorescent, spherical in shape having a lateral size of ca. 5nm. The role of processing parameters such as heating time and ionic strength showed a profound effect on photoluminescence properties of GQDs. The GQDs were N-doped and oxygen-rich as confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Cysteamine hydrochloride (Cys) was used to attach an anti-cancer drug berberine hydrochloride (BHC) on GQDs forming GQDs@Cys-BHC complex with c.a. 88% drug loading efficiency. In vitro drug release was studied at the acidic-basic environment and drug kinetics was studied using pharmacokinetic statistical models. The GQDs were biocompatible on L929 cells whereas theranostic GQDs@Cys-BHC complex showed a potent cytotoxic effect on different cancerous cell line models: cervical cancer cell lines such as HeLa cells and breast cancer cells such as MDA-MB-231 confirmed by Trypan blue and MTT-based cytotoxic assays. Furthermore, multi-excitation based cellular bioimaging was demonstrated using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and fluorescence microscopy using GQDs as well as GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. Thus, drug delivery (therapeutic) and bioimaging (diagnostic) properties of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex are thought to have a potential in vitro theranostic application in cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cancer
Drug delivery
Theranostic
Cell cycle
Apoptosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug attachment and in vitro drug release study
In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular bioimaging
Structural, physicochemical, and compositional analyses of GQDs
In vitro drug release and release kinetics studies
Cellular localization and multi-photon bioimaging analysis
Cell cycle analysis and apoptosis/necrosis study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-illuminated (λex =365nm) green fluorescent GQDs separated post-SDGC (marking showing separated fraction of GQDs). (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum showing spectral behaviour of GQDs (100μg/mL), Cysteamine (linker) attached GQDs (GQDs@Cys) and anti-cancer drug attached linker-GQD complex (GQDs@Cys-BHC). The inset shows normalized absorbance intensity of bare Cys and pristine BHC alone. (c) Comparative fluorescence intensity of the GQDs, GQDs@Cys and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex excited at 365nm.
(a) Powdered GQDs, inset showing normal and UV-illuminated GQDs showing fluorescence and (b) showing TEM image of GQDs with roughly spherical morphology.

(a) Effect of pH on fluorescence properties of GQDs and (b) size distribution of GQDs (based on a minimum of 500 representative nanoparticles), and inset showing effect of heating time on the FL intensity of GQDs.
XPS analysis of GQDs. (a) Survey spectrum and deconvulated high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) O1s.

Synthesis of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. (a) Schematic showing surface passivized GQDs and formation of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex via different inter-molecular interactions, and (b) FTIR spectra of bare GQDs (i), GQDs@Cys complex (ii), Bare BHC (iii) and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex (iv).

Pharmacokinetic models for pH responsive BHC release from GQDs@Cys-BHC complex where data is plotted for (a) Zero-order, (b) First degree, (c) Hixson-Crowell, (d) Higuchi and (e) Korsmeyer-Peppas models. Here, Qo and Qt represent initial loaded concentration of BHC and concentration of BHC released from GQDs@Cys complex respectively; (f) Scatter diagram showing the regression values (R2) for different statistical drug release models.

Comparative cell viability analyses on different cell lines using Trypan blue and MTT assay. Biocompatible nature of bare GQDs (0.2mg/mL) and bare Cys (3000ppm); while the final theranostic complex i.e. GQDs@Cys-BHC (~0.4mM BHC) showed cytotoxicity after 24 and 48h at their respective maximum concentration (n = 3, mean ± SD). Cells showed concentration dependent cell toxicity confirmed by trypan blue (a–c) as well as MTT-based assay (d–f). Compared to pristine or bare BHC, theranostic GQDs@Cys-BHC was more cytotoxic to cells.

CLSM images at two laser excitation wavelengths: 488nm (green) and 550 nm (red) using GQDs@Cys-BHC complex (final concentration; ~200μg/mL GQD concentration in the complex). Images show HeLa cells (a–d), L929 cells (e–h), and MDA-MB-231 cells (i–l) stained with GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. Cellular intake is clearly demonstrated by co-localization merged channels.

Cell cycle analysis and apoptosis with untreated control, and treated pristine BHC or GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. Cell cycle analysis was studied on L929 cells (left lane; a–d) and HeLa cells (right lane; e–h) after 24h. Graphs represent untreated control cells (a, e), cells treated with pristine BHC (0.4mM; b, f) and cells treated with GQDs@Cys-BHC complex (~0.4mM BHC; c, g), and quantitative distribution of cell cycle growth phases (d, h). For apoptosis study on representative cancerous HeLa cells, control (i), with pristine BHC at a concentration of 0.4mM (j) and treated with GQDs@Cys-BHC complex ~0.4mM content (k) are shown. Quantitative percentage of cells in their respective apoptosis stages are given as inset values.

head.ce:title: Milk-derived multi-fluorescent graphene quantum dot-based cancer theranostic system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology and Bioinformatics, D.Y. Patil University, Sector 15, CBD Belapur, Navi Mumbai 400 614, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: N.S.N. Research Centre for Nanotechnology and Bio-nanotechnology, Jambhul Phata, Ambernath (W) 421 504, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Walchand Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Bio-nanotechnology (wcRnb)
Walchand College of Arts and Science

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Walchand Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Bio-nanotechnology (wcRnb), Walchand College of Arts and Science, Walchand-Hirachand Marg, Ashok Chowk, Solapur 413006, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Walchand Centre for Research in Nanotechnology and Bio-nanotechnology (wcRnb)
Walchand College of Arts and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An economical green-chemistry approach was used for the synthesis of aqueous soluble graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from cow milk for simultaneous imaging and drug delivery in cancer. The GQDs synthesized using one-pot microwave-assisted heating were multi-fluorescent, spherical in shape having a lateral size of ca. 5nm. The role of processing parameters such as heating time and ionic strength showed a profound effect on photoluminescence properties of GQDs. The GQDs were N-doped and oxygen-rich as confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. Cysteamine hydrochloride (Cys) was used to attach an anti-cancer drug berberine hydrochloride (BHC) on GQDs forming GQDs@Cys-BHC complex with c.a. 88% drug loading efficiency. In vitro drug release was studied at the acidic-basic environment and drug kinetics was studied using pharmacokinetic statistical models. The GQDs were biocompatible on L929 cells whereas theranostic GQDs@Cys-BHC complex showed a potent cytotoxic effect on different cancerous cell line models: cervical cancer cell lines such as HeLa cells and breast cancer cells such as MDA-MB-231 confirmed by Trypan blue and MTT-based cytotoxic assays. Furthermore, multi-excitation based cellular bioimaging was demonstrated using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and fluorescence microscopy using GQDs as well as GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. Thus, drug delivery (therapeutic) and bioimaging (diagnostic) properties of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex are thought to have a potential in vitro theranostic application in cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer nanomedicine is one of the leading nanotechnology research focuses due to drawbacks in conventional chemotherapy such as target non-selectivity, high toxicity, rapid clearance of chemotherapeutic drugs through metabolism, drug deactivation, multi-drug resistance of tumours and accumulation at non-specific sites [1,2]. Reappearance of regressed tumours followed by chemotherapy or radiation therapy associated with cancer stem cells (CSCs) leads to failure of treatment in patients via conventional chemotherapy routes [3,4]. Use of novel nanomaterials for tumour therapeutics and diagnostics [5,6] especially ultra-small nanoparticles <5nm exhibits quantum confinement effects and possesses unique optical properties [7–9]. Amalgamation of two or more unique properties of nanomaterials in order to engineer unit multi-functional nanomaterial platform for therapy and diagnostics is termed as ‘Theranostic’ or ‘Theragnostic’ system [10–12].

The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. Major properties of GQDs comprise high aqueous/lipophilic solubility, multiple excitation dependent fluorescence, non-blinking florescence, long fluorescent lifetime, and resistant to photobleaching [7–9]. They surpass traditional semi-conductor quantum dots with an economical, environmental friendly precursors, and biocompatibility. The GQDs have been synthesized by different methods such as microwave assisted pyrolysis [13,14], laser ablation [15], and electro-chemical oxidation [16]. Similarly, use of green precursors such as camphor [17], sugars [18], food-wastes [19], phyto-extracts [20–23], overcooked meat [24], and even human urine [25] has been reported for fluorescent carbon nanomaterial synthesis.

Fluorescent carbon is capable of coupling biological entities for loading/unloading therapeutics and exploring their optical properties under external stimuli. A more extensive insight is needed for their application in cancer drug delivery application such as their solubility, biocompatibility, biodegradability, water-dispersibility, drug loading capacity, and sustained drug release. The GQDs have been identified and modulated as theranostic systems for drug delivery [22,26–28]. Berberine is a natural alkaloid present in medicinal plants of Berberis sp. and has been identified for it potent pharmacological role as an anti-inflammatory [29], anti-microbial [30], and even anti-neoplastic and anti-tumorigenic properties [31]. Cytotoxic activity of Berberine is via different mechanisms such as cell cycle arrest, affecting extracellular matrix proteins, and apoptosis in cancer cells [32]. The GQDs-tagged berberine hydrochloride (BHC) system has an advantage of image-guided delivery and tracking of cell treatment. Drug delivery using BHC has been studied for anti-bacterial and anti-cancer applications as well [33–34], therefore BHC-nanoparticle based theranostic system delivers multi-treatment opportunities in addition to anti-cancer drug delivery theranostics.

In this report, we present use of facile green chemistry synthesis protocol for the synthesis of green fluorescent GQDs using cow milk as a precursor, although GQDs using Soy milk has been synthesized and demonstrated for oxygen-reduction catalytic applications albeit with low quantum yield (QY: 2.6%) [35], current report shows GQDs (QY: 25%) for drug delivery and theranostic application. Another recent report shows how green-synthesized GQDs from cow milk along with metallic conjugation provide an excellent platform for anti-bacterial films [36]. Cow milk provides a novel, feasible and economical source for GQDs production. The GQDs were purified, characterized and then functionalized with anti-cancer drug BHC using Cys-HCl (Cys) as a linker molecule. A Cys-HCl is an extremely biocompatible molecule, widely used as a linker for conjugating drug and nanoparticles [37]. It aids in an attachment of BHC drug onto GQDs with high efficiency. An in vitro release of BHC was analysed using various pharmacokinetic models of drug delivery [38]. The effect of bare GQDs and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex on cell cycle was elucidated and their theranostic application was demonstrated in mammalian cells.

Commercially available pasteurized cow milk was purchased from Amul India, Ltd and kept under refrigeration (4°C) until further use. Berberine hydrochloride hydrate (BHC), molecular weight 408.28Da (BHC; 99% purity), cysteamine hydrochloride (Cys; ≥98% purity), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9% purity), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 1M), and ethanol (EtOH, 70%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Propidium iodide (PI, 50μg/mL), RNase A (100μg/mL), and annexin V-FITC conjugate (5μg/mL) were procured from Biolegend®. All glasswares were pre-treated with aqua regia and washed with Milli-Q water to remove traces of any metal ions.

Filtered pasteurized cow milk (50mL) was heated in a domestic microwave (Godrej, Model- GMX 30 CA1 SIM, Power Output 900W, 2450MHz) for a different time duration (20, 40, 60 and 80min). Each residue obtained after heating was collected, to which 20mL NaOH and 10mL EtOH (2:1) was added and stirred constantly for 15min using magnetic stirrer. The resultant solution was observed for fluorescence under UV-illumination (Ex: 365nm). Sucrose density gradient centrifugation (SDGC) was used for purification of nascent carbon dots at same sucrose gradient conditions as reported earlier by Pandey et al. [20]. The fluorescent band under UV-excitation, consisting of GQDs was carefully collected by puncturing the tube using a syringe and centrifuged at 16,770× g for 30min. The pellet was collected and re-dispersed in an equal volume of nanopure water. Powdered form of GQDs was obtained by slow evaporation of the solution in a vacuum oven for 4h and the black colored powder was weighed according to the concentration required for further analysis.

A stock solution of Cys-HCl (3mg/mL); corresponding to 3000ppm was prepared in nanopure water. A 50mL stock solution of GQDs (5mg/mL) was prepared and stored at 4°C. GQDs were functionalized using different concentrations (5, 10, 15, 25, 50, 100 and 150ppm) of Cys-HCl, by adding to 1mg/mL of GQDs. The shift in the spectra was monitored using UV–vis spectrophotometer at regular interval of time till 2h. Each solution was centrifuged at 16,770× g for 30min followed by re-dissolution in the same volume of nanopure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug attachment and in vitro drug release study
ce:section.ce:para: A stock solution of BHC (0.5mM) was prepared by adding 2.04mg BHC in 10mL nanopure water in dark and sterile environment. Nearly 800μL of this stock solution was added to lmL of GQDs@Cys complex (50ppm, ~0.2mg/mL only GQDs) to make a final drug concentration in the complex as (0.4mM).The solution was centrifuged at 16,770× g for 20min and the pellet was collected and re-dissolved in 5mL nanopure water. Attachment study was studied using UV–vis spectroscopy. For in vitro BHC drug release, phosphate buffer saline (PBS) of pH5.8 and pH7.4 were prepared. About 5mL of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex was taken in a dialysis bag of 12kDa MW cut-off and BHC release in PBS at respective pH values was studied by taking absorbance at 346nm as a function of time (1–72h) at 37°C. Drug concentration was determined by using a standard curve with the equation of the line (y=1.28×+0.01). Drug loading efficiency (DLE) was calculated using the formula as given in supplementary information Scheme 1. Drug release profile of BHC from GQDs@Cys-BHC complex was studied using common pharmacokinetic models — Zero-order, First degree, Hixson-Crowell, Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminary cytotoxicity and cellular bioimaging were performed on representative normal cells such as L929 cell line, and cancerous cells: MDA-MB-231 and HeLa cell lines. Cells were procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India. Cells were seeded at a concentration 2×106 cells/mL and kept at 5% CO2 at 37°C. The cytotoxicity of bare or pristine BHC was determined using by Trypan blue and MTT-based assay. The cytotoxic effect of bare GQDs, bare Cys, bare BHC and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex was analysed using ELISA Microplate reader by taking absorbance at 570nm (BioRad Laboratories). For fluorescent imaging of cells, filtered GQDs (Millipore filter 0.22μm pore size) and sterile GQDs@Cys-BHC complex were added to the wells containing cells attached on a cover slip and incubated for 4h at 5% CO2 at 37°C. The medium was then washed with 1% PBS (pH7), the cover slip was carefully mounted on a sterile glass slide for fluorescence or Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) analysis.

Cell cycle and apoptosis studies were performed using flow cytometry on L929 and HeLa cells respectively. Cells were seeded at a concentration of 3×106 cells/mL and were treated with bare GQDs and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. For cell cycle analysis, the cells were dispersed in 500μL PBS (1%, pH7) with PI (50μg/mL) and RNase A (100μg/mL) and kept in dark for 15min before flow cytometry analysis. For apoptosis study, cells were treated with PI (50μg/mL), annexin V-FITC conjugate (5μg/mL) and dispersed in PBS (1%, pH7). Each analysis was performed at least thrice (n =3) and a count of a minimum of 10,000 events was taken for each analysis. Data are represented as mean±S.D.

Dual beam spectrophotometer (Lambda-25, Perkin Elmer, USA) was used for UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy (Perkin-Elmer, USA) using a standard quartz cuvette having a path length of 1cm (Gain voltage: 400V). Morphological details of the GQDs were obtained using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) and Energy Dispersive Analysis of X-ray (EDAX) was performed to find out elemental composition of the sample (Carl Zeiss Microimaging, GmbH, Germany). The powder XRD diffraction patterns were obtained with 40kV Philips PW 3040/60 diffractometer. Scanning angles were from 10° to 80° (2θ) at a 10° per min. Fourier transformed infra-red (FTIR) spectroscopy (Brucker) studies were performed within the spectral window of 500 to 4000cm−1. XPS analysis was performed using a Multilab 2000 Photoelectron Spectrometer. Bioimaging was done using fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany) and CLSM (Olympus, Flowview). Flow cytometry was performed using BD FACSAria™ and analysis was done using FCS Express 5 Flow Plus Denovo Software.

The GQDs synthesized from milk and separated from bulk materials using SDGC exhibited aqueous solubility and bright intense green colored fluorescence band (Fig. 1a) under UV-illumination (365nm). The UV–vis absorption analysis of GQDs showed multiple peaks (Fig. 1b) at 222nm and 270nm arising out of π–π* and n–-π* electronic transitions from CC and CO groups respectively [7–9]. On the other hand, GQDs@Cys showed slight red-shift at absorbance maximum near 225nm and slight diminution at 270nm. This might be due to change in the extinction co-efficient following attachment of Cys onto GQDs leading to aggregation leading to red-shift. Further attachment of BHC to form GQDs@Cys-BHC complex was confirmed by new peaks formed at 215, 260 and 340nm. The changes in intensity and shift in the absorbance maximum are attributed to the stable GQDs@Cys-BHC complex structure formed. Fluorescence (FL) studies showed decreased PL intensity of GQDs after formation of GQDs@Cys and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. GQDs showed a wide emission around 400–500nm after excitation at 375nm which is in accordance with earlier reports on GQDs properties [7]. There was a decrease in fluorescence emission at slight red-shift mostly due to the formation of new complexes via linker-drug coating on defective sites of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, physicochemical, and compositional analyses of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a shows the crude powdered form obtained after heating of the precursor and its inset shows the normal light and UV-illuminated image (λex =365nm) of green fluorescent GQDs (100μg/mL). Structural or morphological characterization of GQDs using HRTEM showed that the GQDs were roughly spherical in shape (Fig. 2b). The GQDs can exist self-assembled forms leading to increase in overall size via π–π conjugation or solvent effect [39,40]. The GQDs however were well dispersed and showed no signs of aggregation (Fig. S1).

Both pH and heating time exhibited a profound effect on the formation of GQDs (Fig. 3 ). The GQDs showed a maximum FL intensity at pH 6.0 and it varies with perturbation in different pH values. Such profound effect of pH on fluorescence intensity could be used as sensors for pH-responsive applications. The FL intensity of the GQDs increased from pH 1.0 to pH 6.0 and decreased dramatically after pH 7 to pH 14. The surface charge of GQDs changes with respect to ionic concentration of the solution. Aggregation and segregation of GQDs lead to changes in the structural assembly of GQDs thereby leading to change in photoluminescence intensity [41]. The size distribution analysis showed the average size of GQDs ~5nm (Fig. 3b and Fig. S1). Monodispersity in lateral size, as seen in metallic nanoparticles, is challenging to obtain in GQDs due to difficulty in controlling the reaction conditions and elemental composition of the reaction substrate in green synthesis approach. Effect of microwave heating time also affects the FL property of GQDs (inset of Fig. 3b) and Fig. S2 shows UV-illuminated images of as-prepared GQD solution. Effect of heating time (inset of Fig. 3b) showed that the optimum time taken for GQDs formation was 60min. Variable percentage elemental composition of GQDs was obtained as a function of heating for different durations (Table S1). Quantum yield (QY) calculations showed highest QY at 60min (Supplementary S4) and thus it was selected for further studies. High stability of GQDs was studied with increasing ionic strength using salt concentration (Fig. S5) making them an ideal candidate for drug delivery.

XPS analysis showed that the GQDs were mainly composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) and each peak was deconvulated and fitted using Lorentzian curve fitting. The GQDs were inherently N-doped and Oxygen-rich in nature without any external addition of reagents (Fig. 4 ). The high resolution C1s XPS spectra (Fig. 4b) was deconvulated into COC (~286eV), CN and CN (~284eV), CO (~287eV), OCO (~288eV), CO (~287eV), MCO3 (~291eV). These oxygen-containing C-atom binds are in accordance with the high resolution O1s spectra as well as FTIR spectra. The N-doping characteristic was in accordance with the N1s XPS spectra (Fig. 4c) which was deconvulated into graphitic CN (~401eV) due to bonding of N to neighbouring three C-atoms and NH2 peak (~402eV). The O1s spectra (Fig. 4d) was deconvulated into three peaks: CO (~533eV), C-O-C (~534eV), OCOH (~535eV). Thus these oxygen-rich GQDs are highly soluble and stable in aqueous solution.

Fig. 5a shows a schematic for oxygen-rich surface passivized characteristic of GQDs and chemistry for the attachment of BHC onto linker-stabilised GQDs. An FTIR spectrum of bare GQDs (Fig. 5b(i)) shows a wide band at 1222cm−1 due to the presence of multiple alkene CH plane bending arising from sp2 carbon and COC stretching. This is in accordance with the C1s XPS analysis. The narrow band at 1634cm−1 represent CC stretch, ketone CO stretch and amine NH bends whereas mild hump at 2823cm−1 and 2900cm−1 relates to carboxylic OH group showing oxygen rich functionalization of GQDs. A relatively narrow intense peak at 3440cm−1 is due to a large number of OH stretching vibrations arising out of the aqueous part in which the GQDs were dispersed. Attachment of Cys showed a shift in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S6). IR spectrum of GQDs@Cys (Fig. 5b, ii) showed bands at 580cm−1 arising fromCCl stretches and 722cm−1 due to CH2 and CH bends. The feeble band at 1442cm−1 represents CC stretch and carboxylic OH bend. Band at 1591cm−1 shows amide NH bend arising probably due to GQDs@Cys complex formation. IR bands at 3426cm−1, 3706cm−1 and 3785cm−1 are due to alcohol and carboxylic O H bonds contributing towards weak electrostatic interactions such as hydrogen bonding in the complex. Multiple bands were obtained for bare BHC as at 680cm−1 for alkane CH2 bend, alkene and aromatic CH bend, and CCl stretches from the drug backbone. A strong band at 1021cm−1 is due to alcohol CO stretch and COC stretch whereas bands at 1525 cm−1 and 1642 cm−1 are due to aromatic CC stretches. Wide bands at 3443, 3745 and 3866 cm−1 are due to alcohol and carboxyl OH stretch from aqueous solution. The GQDs@Cys-BHC complex (Fig. 5b (iv)) is formed via such as disulfide bonding (formation of new wide band at 600cm−1), amide bond between GQDs@Cys and BHC (intense band at 1642cm−1) and fewer isocyanate stretch (NCO, a new band at 2063cm−1). Wide band beyond 3000 cm−1 suggests that weak hydrogen bonding might also provide extensive stability since GQDs@Cys-BHC complex was soluble in aqueous medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release and release kinetics studies
ce:section.ce:para: Drug loading efficiency for GQDs@Cys-BHC complex was calculated to be 88% (Supplementary Scheme 1). In vitro drug release profile shows sustained drug release of BHC from GQDs@Cys-BHC complex in a pH-responsive manner (Fig S7). A pH-responsive drug delivery is central in the engineering of drug delivery vehicles since the tumour microenvironment has acidic pH [42]. Slightly acidic pH of 5.8 also mimics acidic lysosomes and pH7.4 the normal extracellular environment. At pH7.4, ~0.1mM drug was released at 10h with rapid burst followed by sustained release till 72h. On the other hand, at pH5.8, slow drug release was observed till 3h (~0.02mM), followed by relatively rapid release till 24h (~0.24mM) and then finally flat plateau of sustained release till 72h. The drug release is due to the breaking of bonds at acidic pH values.

This release pattern inspired us to understand the release of BHC using various statistical drug release models. Fig. 6a–e, shows the pattern of drug release from GQDs@Cys-BHC complex using various equations (Supplementary Scheme 2). Typically, those models having regression coefficient (R2) or goodness of fit closest to 1.0 were assumed to be significant and it was concluded that GQDs@Cys-BHC complex exhibit such drug release behaviour at the respective pH values. Fig. 6f shows that at pH7.4, the complex follows both First-degree and Higuchi release models whereas pH5.8, follows only Higuchi model, based on the fact that these models have R2-value closest to unity. Drug delivery formulations with a first-degree release behaviour could be used to explain the solubility of drugs. Higuchi model is used to explain unidirectional, sustained release of drug which is ideal for drug delivery. It is also characterized by an initial low concentration of drug and diffusion of drugs [38].

Cytotoxicity was performed on normal mouse fibroblast L929 cells, and cancerous HeLa and MDA-MB-231 cell lines for initial in vitro cytotoxicity of BHC, GQDs, Cys, and GQDs@Cys-BHC complex (Fig. 7 and Fig. S8). Both Trypan blue (dye-exclusion test) and MTT assay were used for accounting cytotoxic effect of nanoparticles for after 24 and 48h exposure in order compare the cytotoxic effects via different methods. Fig. 7 shows comparative cytotoxic effect as a function of the concentration of BHC using both the methods. Trypan blue is based on dye-exclusion principle which stains only dead cells having permeability for the dye whereas MTT assay is based on mitochondrial succinate enzyme activity which converts pale yellow MTT dye to violet formazan crystals. Cells' morphology changed from attached branched morphology to round and suspended upon treatment with pristine or bare BHC or GQDs@Cys-BHC complex with an equal concentration of drug (Figs. S9–S11). The GQDs were biocompatible in all the cells for 24h and 48h with a high concentration (200μg/mL). This concentration is very high yet biocompatible as compared to other clinically relevant concentration of other fluorescent molecules required for cellular bioimaging. On comparing bare BHC and theranostic GQDs@Cys-BHC complex, the higher cytotoxic effect was shown in MDA-MB-231 cells, followed by L929 cells and then HeLa cells. The changes in cytotoxic behaviour might be due to changes in cellular uptake dynamics and cellular microenvironment. Nevertheless, nearly 50% cell death was observed after 24h and 48h in L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells was due to BHC drug action by drug release mechanism which was elucidated earlier by in vitro studies. Low HeLa cytotoxicity might be due to slow release or large retention of BHC and slow action after 48h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular localization and multi-photon bioimaging analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs@Cys-BHC complex is a pH sensitive drug-carrier as well as bioimaging system in addition to bare GQDs (Fig. S11). Cellular localization was confirmed using multi-photon CLSM analysis. Fig. 8 shows the fluorescence imaging of cells under normal light and multi-excitation wavelengths. Toxicity of carbon-based nanomaterials has recently been investigated where a size dependent parameter plays a very important role [43,44]. However, in other studies, GQDs have been shown to be biocompatible till high concentrations and potent bioimaging probes with low concentrations [45–51]. The GQDs or drug carrier system might have entered the cells either via receptor-mediated, clathrin- mediated or caveolae-mediated endocytosis [52]. Thus, milk derived GQDs served excellent nanoprobes for imaging of cells even at low concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cycle analysis and apoptosis/necrosis study
ce:section.ce:para: Cell cycle and apoptosis were performed to understand the effect of theranostic GQDs@Cys-BHC complex. Fig. 9 (a-h) shows cell cycle analysis of untreated control cells, treated bare BHC or GQDs@Cys-BHC complex on normal L929 cells and cancerous HeLa cells. As compared to control, G1-phase in cells drastically increased after the treatment of BHC either in pristine form or in theranostic complex (Fig. 9 d and h). Thus, a G1 cell arrest was found in both normal and cancerous cells. Recently, Gu et al. studied cell cycle and apoptosis effect of bare BHC on L929 cells [53]. They concluded that the main mechanism of cell death in these cells was apoptosis. Thus, to understand the effect GQDs@Cys-BHC of cell death, we performed apoptosis study on HeLa cells (Fig. 9i–k). Untreated control cells were viable after 24h (Fig. 9i) whereas cells treated with GQDs@Cys-BHC complex (Fig. 9j and k) showed almost all cells entered late apoptosis with very few in necrosis stage. Qualitative analysis by PI staining of apoptotic cells has been shown in Fig. S12. This is in accordance with studies on L929 cells where most cells were found to enter apoptosis in concentration-dependent manner [54]. The GQDs@Cys-BHC complex entered the cell where BHC was released and caused the cell death via apoptosis. Fig. 8 shows the bioimaging “diagnostic” capability of GQDs (alone or in a complex) along with the “therapeutic” action (shown in Fig. 9).

In comparison to previously reported carbon based theranostic therapy [13,26,25], GQDs from a green source like a cow milk provide a novel platform for further development in therapy and diagnostic areas. Current study encouraged the theranostic potential of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex both as a drug delivery and multi-fluorescent imaging probe in vitro in cancer. It would be interesting to understand the bioactivity of GQDs@Cys-BHC complex with the attachment of a tumour marker such as folic acid and study its role as active drug targeting probe in normal as well as cancer cell lines. Also, the complex provides an opportunity for attachment of different anti-cancer drugs either alone or combinational therapy for further in vitro and in vivo studies.

Cow milk proved to be a good precursor for the synthesis of bright multi-fluorescent GQDs. These GQDs were used for loading an anti-cancer drug using cysteamine hydrochloride as a linker (GQDs@Cys-BHC). Pharmacokinetic study of a release of the drug showed that it follows different model depending on the pH e.g. at pH7.4, the complex follows both First-degree and Higuchi release models whereas pH5.8, follows only Higuchi model. Moreover, GQDs are demonstrated to be a suitable drug-ferrying vehicle as well as for dual-fluorescent nanoprobes even at low concentration. Flow cytometry based analysis confirmed the G1-phase arrest and apoptosis mechanism of cell death. These findings demand further detailed study of the role of GQDs in cellular imaging, therapeutics and future medicine.

M.T., A.M., S·P. and M.S. designed the experiments on GQDs synthesis and drug delivery studies. M.T., M.B. and K·S. have equal contributions in designing and conducting cell cytotoxicity assays and bioimaging. M.T. performed confocal imaging and flow cytometry based studies. M.T., M.S., and M.S. wrote the manuscript. Authors declare no conflict of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Prof. Rohit Srivastava for providing characterization facilities at Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF) Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Bombay and Electron Spectrometer for Chemical Analysis (ESCA) facility at Department of Physics, IIT-Bombay India.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EDS analysis, elemental composition, Raman spectra, XRD study, QY calculations,linker conjugation, drug attachment calculations and release studies, ion stability study, cytotoxicity assay, cellular morphological analysis, fluorescence imaging, and PI-staining based apoptosis study.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2016.05.007.
